name: scrapeURL engine fallback pattern
applicability: Direct transferable architecture pattern for SoNash website-analysis handler. Current website-analysis uses superpowers-chrome as the single extraction path; firecrawl's fallback chain (fetch → fire-engine → playwright → wikipedia) is a proven pattern for handling heterogeneous web content.
home_connection: SoNash T28 website-analysis handler (.claude/skills/website-analysis/) would benefit from a documented fallback list with explicit failure semantics (NoEnginesLeftError equivalent).

name: Test gating by runtime capability
applicability: Gate tests by env-var capability (TEST_SUITE_SELF_HOSTED, OPENAI_API_KEY, OLLAMA_BASE_URL) rather than skip-in-CI fences. Self-hosted users run the same test files and hit the locally-available subset. Directly portable to SoNash's test suite which has a similar hosted-vs-local split.
home_connection: SoNash tests mock httpsCallable — but integration/E2E for Firebase Functions would benefit from this capability-gated pattern (FIREBASE_EMULATOR_HOST, GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS).

name: pnpm harness jest — services-before-tests boot
applicability: Harness owns API + worker boot order before Jest runs. CLAUDE.md explicitly warns not to pnpm start manually. Pattern: a single entry point that boots all local dependencies deterministically before the test runner.
home_connection: SoNash test:build pipeline could adopt a harness script that owns Firebase emulator + Next.js dev server boot. Currently these are started ad-hoc.

name: 58-example cookbook as product marketing
applicability: Instead of a docs/cookbook.md, ship 58 self-contained example apps — each pairing firecrawl with a specific LLM (Claude, GPT, Gemini, Llama, Mistral, DeepSeek, Grok, o1/o3/o4) or framework (openai_swarm, e2b, kubernetes). Each example is ~50-150 lines. Gets new users productive faster than prose docs.
home_connection: SoNash repo-analysis and website-analysis could each have a .research/examples/ directory showing concrete worked examples against known repos/sites. Pattern also applies to JASON-OS: ship example OS configurations for specific AI directors.

name: Multi-agent orchestration with swarm
applicability: Pattern for coordinating multiple agents around a single scraping task. Informs SoNash's agent teams work (audit-review-team, research-plan-team in .claude/teams/) and the gsd-* agent ecosystem.
home_connection: SoNash has 34 agents and 2 teams. openai_swarm_firecrawl is a reference implementation for agent coordination patterns that could inform team boundary design for upcoming JASON-OS team refactoring.

name: Deep Research API usage pattern
applicability: Shows how firecrawl's Deep Research API is invoked from a Python CLI with Claude 3.7 as the reasoning layer. Pattern: interactive preference gathering → deep research call → LLM analysis → structured JSON output.
home_connection: SoNash has its own /deep-research skill with parallel searcher agents. Apartment-finder is a simpler single-agent variant worth comparing — does firecrawl's Deep Research API provide anything SoNash's searcher pipeline doesn't?

name: GitHub repo analyzer example
applicability: Same domain as SoNash's /repo-analysis skill. Worth reading to extract the prompting pattern and see how firecrawl's Map API is used to walk a GitHub repo.
home_connection: Directly comparable to SoNash repo-analysis skill (.claude/skills/repo-analysis/). Compare extraction approaches.

name: Docs-to-OpenAPI pattern
applicability: Convert documentation sites into OpenAPI specs via scraping. Informs SoNash's document-analysis handler — a documentation site IS a kind of structured document.
home_connection: SoNash document-analysis could add a 'extract API contract' mode for docs-to-OpenAPI conversion, using this example as a reference.

name: v2 OpenAPI spec
applicability: Full wire contract for firecrawl's v2 API. Taxonomy: Search / Scrape / Crawl / Map / Extract / Interact / Agent. Worth cataloging the endpoint shapes for reference if SoNash ever needs a scraping contract.
home_connection: Reference for designing httpsCallable contract shapes. Firecrawl's endpoint taxonomy is a mature naming convention for web-data operations.

name: Commercial-OSS split via .env features
applicability: Fire-engine is hosted-only. Self-hosted users get limited CAPTCHA handling unless they configure proxies manually. Pattern: tier features by backing service availability, document the split explicitly, keep the code path the same.
home_connection: If SoNash ever offers a hosted variant (unlikely per privacy-first stance, but noted), this is the canonical pattern for documenting the hosted-only subset.

name: Redlock distributed locking for cache updates
applicability: Pattern for serialising distributed cache mutations across multiple pods. Used for Auth Credit Usage Chunk (ACUC) updates to prevent two workers from racing on the same cache slot.
home_connection: SoNash is single-tenant (Firebase) so distributed locking doesn't apply directly. But the redlock pattern could inform cross-session state coordination in multi-locale setups (two Claude instances editing the same .claude/state/ file).

name: LLM-specific example apps (grouped)
applicability: ~40 examples pairing firecrawl with non-Claude LLMs. Interesting catalog but SoNash is Claude-first. Not actionable beyond knowing they exist.
home_connection: Reference only. If SoNash ever adds multi-model support, re-score these.

name: 7-SDK polyglot coverage
applicability: Firecrawl ships SDKs in 7 languages. Pattern: OpenAPI spec as single source of truth, manual parity enforcement via test-suite. SoNash is TypeScript-only and Firebase-native; polyglot isn't a goal.
home_connection: None currently. Could inform JASON-OS packaging if OS needs per-language installers.

name: No architectural docs/ directory
applicability: Firecrawl has one mermaid diagram (scrapeURL README) and no architecture prose docs. Queue layout, auth flow, service boundaries, API versioning rationale — all have to be inferred from code. New contributors pay the cost.
home_connection: SoNash has extensive docs/ (including AI_WORKFLOW, AGENT_ORCHESTRATION, SECURITY). This is the right choice — keep it. Use firecrawl as evidence of the cost of skipping architecture docs in a fast-moving project.
